conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children. Alex, who's 17, and Blake, who's 3.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine eligibility for both Alex (17) and Blake (3).

Let me ask you a few questions to assess your situation. First:

Are you responsible for Alex and Blake? That means you live with them and are paying towards their upbringing (for example, food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for both of them. They both live with me and I provide for all their needs - food, clothes, everything they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. Now I need to ask about your residency status:

Do you live in the UK, and are you a UK citizen or have the right to live in the UK (such as having settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or qualifying immigration status)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester, and yes, I have the right to live here.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about Alex specifically, since they're 17:

Is Alex currently in approved education or training? This would include things like:
- Being enrolled in full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels, Scottish Highers, or equivalent)
- Being on an approved training program

This is important because Child Benefit can continue past age 16 up until age 20 if the young person is in qualifying education or training.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. He finished school but isn't doing A-levels or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask about Blake now:

Is Blake living with you full-time, or does Blake spend time living with anyone else (such as another parent or family member)?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to make sure I have all the information I need.

Is Blake currently in hospital or in the care of local authorities for an extended period (more than 12 weeks)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in hospital or in care. He's at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake based on the UK government rules.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

Based on the information you've provided:

**For Blake (age 3): ✓ ELIGIBLE**
You can claim Child Benefit for Blake as you meet all the requirements - you're responsible for them, they live with you in the UK, they're under 16, and nobody else is claiming for them.

**For Alex (age 17): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex. While Child Benefit can continue for young people aged 16-19 (under 20), this only applies if they are in approved education or training, such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or approved training programs. Since Alex has finished school and is not currently enrolled in any approved education or training, they don't meet the eligibility criteria.

**Important note:** If Alex decides to return to education or start an approved training program in the future, you could become eligible to claim Child Benefit for them at that point (up until they turn 20).

You can proceed to apply for Child Benefit for Blake. If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and not currently in approved education or training. According to UK Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for children under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you can still qualify if they are under 20 AND staying in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or approved training programs). Since Alex has finished school and is not doing A-levels or any other approved education or training, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 3 years old, which is under the age limit of 16. You are responsible for Blake (living with them and providing for their needs including food and clothes). Blake lives with you full-time in the UK, is not in hospital or care for an extended period, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. You live in the UK and have the right to live here. Blake meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake (age 3) as you meet all the requirements: you're responsible for them, they live with you in the UK, they're under 16, and nobody else is claiming for them. However, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex (age 17) because, while the age limit can extend to 20 for those in approved education or training, Alex is not currently enrolled in any approved education or training program.
